summary:
The fall of the Tokugawa Shogunate — the Bakumatsu period (roughly 1853–1868) — was a time of acute political crisis in Japan, triggered by the forced opening of the country by Western powers and culminating in the overthrow of the military government that had ruled Japan for over 250 years. It gave way to the [[event:2544|Meiji Restoration]] and the radical transformation of Japanese society and government.

The Tokugawa system had enforced a policy of sakoku (closed country) since the early seventeenth century, restricting foreign trade and contact to a narrow channel through the Dutch enclave at Nagasaki. This isolation, combined with a rigid social hierarchy freezing the positions of samurai, peasants, artisans, and merchants, gave Japan over two centuries of peace and internal stability but left the country technologically behind the rapidly industrializing West.

The [[event:2589|Perry Expedition]] of 1853–54, when American Commodore Matthew Perry's steam-powered warships appeared in Edo Bay and demanded that Japan open its ports, demonstrated with shocking clarity the military helplessness of the shogunate. The subsequent Convention of Kanagawa and unequal treaties humiliated Japan in ways that echoed what had happened to China after the [[event:2540|Opium Wars]]. The shogunate's inability to resist Western demands delegitimized it in the eyes of the emperor-revering domains of Satsuma and Choshu, whose samurai rallied under the slogan 'sonnō jōi' — revere the emperor, expel the barbarians.

After a period of civil conflict and political maneuvering, anti-shogunate forces restored imperial rule in the [[event:2590|Boshin War]] of 1868–69. The shogun Tokugawa Yoshinobu resigned his authority and the Meiji emperor was proclaimed the ruler of Japan in what was presented as a 'restoration' of imperial power. The Tokugawa period's end marked the beginning of Japan's rapid modernization and its transformation into a major world power.
